prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
Okay, picking something:

You probably believe you're good at telling when you're being lied to. Most people do. The research on this is brutal and consistent — untrained people detect lies at about 54% accuracy, barely above chance. Even "experts" like police and judges usually hover around the same range, while being far more confident than civilians.

The reason isn't that liars are clever. It's that the cues people trust — avoiding eye contact, fidgeting, hesitation — aren't actually correlated with lying. They're correlated with *nervousness*, which honest people also have, especially when accused.

So if you're reading this thinking "sure, but I really am good at it" — that confidence itself is one of the better-documented parts of the effect.
